coredata.dc:title: Inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by carbon quantum dots: Influencing factors and their application as templates
coredata.dc:creator: Zhai, Xiangang
Gao, Jianping
Wang, Xiaoxue
Mei, Shunkang
Zhao, Ruiru
Wu, Yongli
Hao, Chaoyue
Yang, Jiangbing
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have received a great deal of attention owing to their outstanding physicochemical characteristics and versatile applications. CQDs were employed to stabilize water/oil inverse Pickering emulsions and diethyl-o-phthalate (DEP) was the most suitable oil phase solvent. The stability and average droplet size of the CQD-stabilized inverse Pickering emulsions depended on several factors including sonication time, CQDs concentration, the DEP/water ratio and pH. Additionally, adding an electrolyte to the CQDs aqueous solution greatly enhanced the stability of the inverse Pickering emulsions and decreased the emulsion droplet size. In fact, the addition of Mg2+ significantly improved the stability of the emulsions so that they were stable for more than 4 months. Furthermore, the best conditions for obtaining optimal emulsion were confirmed. In addition, Ag/CQDs microspheres with average size of less than 1 μm were achieved using inverse Pickering emulsions as templates. These microspheres exhibited excellent catalytic performance for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4. This scheme of confining reactants to tiny pools of water is a facile and green synthetic strategy which has the potential to open new avenues for the design and fabrication of a variety of CQD-based hybrid materials and various water soluble polymers with different nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inverse Pickering emulsions
Amphiphilic property
Carbon quantum dots
Smaller Pickering emulsion templates
4-Nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Factors that influence inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs
The catalytic performance of Ag/CQDs microspheres for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China ( 51573126 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photos of inverse Pickering emulsions with different organic solvents (continuous phase) taken 24 h after the preparation of the emulsions. Organic solvents and their respective mass densities at 20 °C: diethyl phthalate (DEP) (1.120 g cm−3) (a), cyclohexane (0.779 g cm−3) (b), liquid paraffin (0.877 g cm−3) (c), trichloromethane (1.484 g cm−3) (d), benzene (0.879 g cm−3) (e), benzyl chloride (1.100 g cm−3) (f), n-hexane (0.659 g cm−3) (g), n-decanol (0.829 g cm−3) (h), ethyl oenanthate (0.882 g cm−3) (i), bromobenzene (1.495 g cm−3) (j), ethyl acetate (0.902 g cm−3) (k), and kerosene (0.800 g cm−3) (l). CQDs concentration: 1 mg mL−1, oil/water ratio: 2:1, sonication time: 15 min.

Optical micrographs and photos (insets) of inverse Pickering emulsions containing different organic solvents (taken 24 h after formation). DEP (a), liquid paraffin (b), benzyl chloride (c) and bromobenzene (d). CQDs concentration: 1 mg mL−1, ultrasonic time: 15 min, oil/water ratio: 2:1. Optical micrographs of inverse Pickering emulsion dyed with Sudan III (e). Inset: inverse Pickering emulsion droplets in water.

Change of average droplet size with ultrasonic time (a), CQDs concentrations: 1 mg mL−1 and DEP/water ratios: 2:1. Changes in average droplet size with CQDs concentration (b). Changes in the volume fractions of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by different concentrations of the CQDs with time (c), the initial volume of the emulsion was used as a reference.
Average droplet size as a function of DEP volume fraction (V0 ) (a). Emulsion fraction (V) as a function of V0 (b). For both V and V0 , the total volume (DEP and water) was used as a reference.

Zeta potentials of aqueous solutions containing 1 mg mL−1 CQDs at different pH values (a). Average droplet size as a function of pH (b). Changes of the volume fractions in the emulsions prepared at different pH values with time (c), the initial volume of the inverse Pickering emulsion was used as a reference.

Zeta potentials of aqueous solutions containing 1 mg mL−1 CQDs and different NaCl or MgCl2 concentrations (a). Changes in average droplet size with time for emulsions prepared with different NaCl and MgCl2 concentrations (b). Changes in volume fractions with time of emulsions prepared with different NaCl or MgCl2 concentrations (c-d), the initial volume of the inverse Pickering emulsion was used as a reference.

Optical micrographs of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs (24 h after preparation). MgCl2 concentrations: 1.5 mM (a), 15 mM (b), 30 mM (c), 50 mM (d), 100 mM (e), 300 mM (f), 500 mM (g) and 1000 mM (h). CQDs concentration: 1 mg mL−1, DEP/water ratio: 2:1, ultrasonic time: 15 min. Changes in average droplet size with MgCl2 concentrations for inverse Pickering emulsions (i).
Photos of the inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs with different MgCl2 concentrations at various time intervals after the formation of the emulsions.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs and the Ag/CQDs microspheres in aqueous solutions. Inset: optical images of aqueous solutions of CQDs and Ag/CQDs under visible light (a). The XRD pattern of the Ag/CQDs microspheres (b).
XPS full-range spectrum of the as-prepared Ag/CQDs microspheres (a). C 1s (b), N 1s (c), O 1s (d) and Ag 3d (e) high-resolution core level spectra of the as-prepared Ag/CQDs microspheres.
SEM images of the Ag/CQDs microspheres prepared using inverse Pickering emulsion templates (a-b). Particle size distribution of the Ag/CQDs (c). Solid curve is the Gaussian fit.
TEM images of the Ag/CQDs microspheres (a-b). High magnification of an Ag nanoparticle (c). EDS spectrum of the Ag/CQDs microspheres (d).
Preparation route for the inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs (A). Schematic illustration of the fabrication of Ag/CQDs microspheres using the inverse Pickering emulsions (B).
Schematic illustration of hydrogen bonding and “π − π” interactions between the CQDs and the DEP aromatic molecules.
Schematic illustration of the protonation and dissociation of the functional groups on the surface of CQDs as a function of pH.

head.ce:title: Inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by carbon quantum dots: Influencing factors and their application as templates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have received a great deal of attention owing to their outstanding physicochemical characteristics and versatile applications. CQDs were employed to stabilize water/oil inverse Pickering emulsions and diethyl-o-phthalate (DEP) was the most suitable oil phase solvent. The stability and average droplet size of the CQD-stabilized inverse Pickering emulsions depended on several factors including sonication time, CQDs concentration, the DEP/water ratio and pH. Additionally, adding an electrolyte to the CQDs aqueous solution greatly enhanced the stability of the inverse Pickering emulsions and decreased the emulsion droplet size. In fact, the addition of Mg2+ significantly improved the stability of the emulsions so that they were stable for more than 4 months. Furthermore, the best conditions for obtaining optimal emulsion were confirmed. In addition, Ag/CQDs microspheres with average size of less than 1 μm were achieved using inverse Pickering emulsions as templates. These microspheres exhibited excellent catalytic performance for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4. This scheme of confining reactants to tiny pools of water is a facile and green synthetic strategy which has the potential to open new avenues for the design and fabrication of a variety of CQD-based hybrid materials and various water soluble polymers with different nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: Pickering emulsions have aroused ongoing interest due to their extensive applications in numerous fields, including food engineering and pharmaceuticals [1], microencapsulation and material processing [2,3], adsorption of toxic metal ions [4], polymerization [5], and the simulation of living systems to investigate polystage cascade reactions [6]. Pickering emulsions can be divided into O/W and W/O (inverse) two types, in Pickering emulsions, two immiscible phases are stabilized solely by solid particles [7]. Compared to conventional emulsions which are stabilized by organic surfactants, Pickering emulsions are much more stable against coalescence. This is due to a decrease of the total interfacial energy and the strong quasi-irreversible adsorption of solid particles at the interfaces between the two liquids or liquid-gas phases [8–12]. In addition, the use of solid particles as stabilizers has added advantages such as being cost-effective and eco-friendly [1,5]. A vast range of solid particles such as graphene oxide [1], silica nanoparticles [13–15], TiO2 [16], holocellulose nanocrystals [17], chitin nanocrystals [18], Janus particles [19] and clay [20] have been used as emulsifiers for Pickering emulsions. Unfortunately, the diameters of the Pickering emulsion droplets stabilized by the above-mentioned stabilizers are commonly in the μm range, and the materials prepared from these large Pickering emulsion templates have poor catalytic properties [1]. So far, Pickering emulsion studies are primarily focused on O/W emulsions which cannot be used for syntheses in aqueous phase [1,11]. However, many inorganic functional materials and water soluble polymers (e.g. polyacrylamide microspheres) can only be synthesized in aqueous phase. However, few studies relate inverse Pickering emulsion for aqueous phase synthesis. This situation greatly limits the development and application of Pickering emulsion.

In general, small size Pickering emulsions can be prepared from small solid particles [11]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are defined as quasi-spherical nanoparticles (zero-dimensional) with sizes smaller than 10 nm [21]. They are non-toxic, inexpensive, biocompatible and photoluminescent [22]. In recent years, CQDs have been used for versatile applications such as bioimaging [23], photocatalysis [24], supercapacitors and fuel cells [25,26], selective ion detection [27,28], photovoltaic devices [29], drug delivery [30], and optoelectronics [31]. CQDs are composed of a hydrophobic carbon core which primarily contains CC bonds [32], whereas the surfaces contain abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups [33]. This amphiphilic nature should make CQD an ideal green emulsifier to stabilize Pickering emulsions. However, the preparation of the inverse Pickering emulsion using CQD as a stabilizer and the use of the resulting emulsion as a template for preparation of CQD-based composites has not been reported.

The toxic and carcinogenic compound 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is widely used in insecticides, dyes and herbicides [34,35]. It is a serious threat to the human beings and animals because it can injure the liver, kidney and the central nervous system and it causes skin and eye irritation [34,36–38]. Hence, extensive efforts have been made to remove 4-NP via various methods, including adsorption, membrane filtration [39]. The most practical method is the catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP (4-aminophenol) using NaBH4. Several noble metals including Ag, Au and Pt nanoparticles have been used as catalysts for this reaction [38]. Among these catalysts, Ag is the most cost-effective and has a high catalytic activity. Unfortunately, the irreversible aggregation of Ag nanoparticles leads to a dramatic reduction in catalytic performance [40–42]. To overcome this drawback, noble metals are always dispersed on various supports like carbon materials, polymers and metal oxides [38]. In addition, the syntheses of some composite catalysts are complicated, time-consuming and energy intensive [35]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, fast and efficient method for preparing high-performance composite catalysts.

Herein, a novel stabilizer CQDs (without surface modification) and a novel oil solvent DEP were used to fabricate inverse Pickering emulsions (Scheme 1 A), and then a one-pot approach to synthesize Ag/CQDs microspheres based on the inverse Pickering emulsion was also developed (Scheme 1B). This method possesses lots of advantages such as simple, fast, green, facile and energy-saving. The assembled Ag/CQDs microspheres with average size of less than 1 μm displayed excellent catalytic performance for the reduction of 4-NP in the presence of NaBH4. Meanwhile, the factors that influence the stability and average droplet size of the inverse Pickering emulsions were also investigated. Moreover, compared to other solid particles, using CQDs to stabilize Pickering emulsions has following advantages: (a) CQDs have smaller particle diameters (about 2–10 nm) than graphene oxide (several μm) or other solid particles; (b) CQDs are easily prepared (microwave oven for 150 s); (c) CQDs can stabilize smaller Pickering emulsion droplets (average diameters of about 500 nm for CQD-stabilized emulsions compared to several μm for those stabilized by graphene oxide and other solid particles). This work can provide a green, fast, simple and facile synthetic strategy to construct CQD-based hybrid materials or water-soluble polymers with nanostructures by confining the reactants in tiny water pools.

DEP was obtained from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co. Ltd. All other reagents (triammonium citrate (ACA), trisodium phosphate (TSP), 4-nitrophenol, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, sodium chloride, sodium borohydride, silver nitrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, the organic solvents, and Sudan III biological stain) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used as received.

The synthesis of CQDs and Ag/CQDs microspheres, preparation of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs, reduction of 4-nitrophenol using NaBH4 catalyzed by Ag/CQDs microspheres, and materials characterization were shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors that influence inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of the as-synthesized CQDs was shown in Supporting Information.

Among the twelve solvents (Fig. 1), the aromatic organic solvents (Fig. 1a, f and j) were more suitable than the non-aromatic organic solvents (Fig. 1b-d, g-i, k and l) for the fabrication of inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs. This is due to “π − π” conjugation between the aromatic rings and the conjugated system in the CQDs [43]. Among the non-aromatic organic solvents, liquid paraffin formed the best inverse Pickering emulsion (Fig. 1c). This may be due to the presence of residual aromatic hydrocarbons which are generated during the refining process of paraffin oil. Abnormally, benzene did not form a good emulsion (Fig. 1e) which may be due to the lack of substituent groups on its aromatic ring. This led to weaker interactions between the CQDs and the benzene solvent.

The optical micrographs of the inverse Pickering emulsions from the four best organic solvents are shown in Fig. 2a-d. DEP gave the densest emulsion droplets (Fig. 2a). Thus, DEP is the optimal oil phase for the synthesis of inverse Pickering emulsions. This is due to two types of interactions. First, there are “π − π” conjugations between the aromatic rings of DEP and the conjugated domains in the CQDs. Second, each DEP molecule had two carbonyl oxygen atoms which can serve as receptors for hydrogen bonds [44]. This provides many interactions between the CQDs and DEP, as illustrated in Scheme 2 .

Next the effect of ultrasonic time on the morphology of the inverse Pickering emulsion droplets was investigated. The ultrasonic power was kept at 200 W. The optical micrographs and size distribution histograms (insets) are shown in Fig. S7a-f. In general, as the ultrasonic time increased, the average size of the emulsion droplets decreased from 490 to 370 nm and the size distributions became narrower and more uniform. There are two reasons for this phenomenon. First, at longer sonication times, more energy was introduced into the emulsification system, which was helpful for the migration of the CQDs from the aqueous solution to the interfaces between the water and the DEP. As a result, more dense emulsion droplets were formed. This is further confirmed by the color change of the emulsions shown in Fig. S6a-f. The inverse Pickering emulsions turned from clear to milky white as the sonication time increased. The second reason for smaller droplets at longer sonication times is that sonication helps to break up aggregated CQDs. However it should be noted that the average droplet size did not change significantly for sonication times longer than 5 min (Fig. 3 a), indicating that there is a limit to how much sonication can reduce the size of the emulsion droplets.

The concentration of solid particles is another important factor that affect emulsion stability and average emulsion droplet size. Photographs and optical micrographs of inverse Pickering emulsions prepared with different CQDs particle concentrations are shown in Figs. S8 and S9. Fig. 3b and c show the changes of the average droplet sizes as a function of CQDs concentrations and the changes in the emulsion fractions over time. In both ordinary emulsions formed by conventional surfactants and Pickering emulsions, the average droplet size usually decreases as the particle emulsifier (or surfactant) concentration increases. However, above a certain particle concentration, the droplet size will remain essentially constant [45]. As expected, this trend was observed for increasing concentrations of CQDs; the average droplet size first decreased from 490 to 320 nm (bottom right insets in Fig. S9a-f) and then remained fairly constant at higher CQDs concentrations (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the size distributions of the droplets became narrower as the CQDs concentration increased (optical micrographs and bottom right insets in Fig. S9a-f). In addition, increasing the CQDs concentration also promoted emulsion stability as demonstrated the slower decrease in volume fractions with time in the emulsions stabilized by higher concentrations of CQDs (Fig. 3c). Increasing CQDs concentration within a certain range may cause more CQDs to assemble at the DEP-water interface, which is conducive for stabilizing larger areas of the liquid–liquid interface and which reduces the surface energy of the system and consequently facilitates the formation of more stable emulsions.

The sizes of the emulsion droplets and the emulsion volumes also depend on the volume ratios of DEP/water. Fig. S10 shows the inverse Pickering emulsions prepared at different DEP phase fractions (V0 ). Increasing V0 results in larger emulsion droplets and also leads to an increase in the nonuniformity of the droplet shapes (Fig. 4 a, optical micrographs and bottom right insets in Fig. S11a-f). In addition, Fig. 4b shows that the emulsion volume fractions (V) increases linearly with V0 , indicating that V is determined by V0 . For emulsions with lower water contents, fewer CQDs particles were able to stabilize the droplets. Consequently, there were too few CQDs adsorbed at the interface of DEP and water to form a complete interfacial film, thus, the small droplets tended to coalesce into larger ones.

The pH can influence the electrical properties and the surface wetting characteristics of solid particles containing ionogenic functional groups, which will affect the average droplet size and the stability of Pickering emulsions [1]. Photos of inverse Pickering emulsions formed at different pH values are shown in Fig. S12a-g. Zeta potentials can be used to describe the electrical properties of CQDs and the Zeta potentials of aqueous CQDs solutions at different pH values are shown in Fig. 5 a. The Zeta potential of the CQDs solution at pH = 6.7 was −4.5 mV, indicating a negatively charged property. As the pH increased, the Zeta potentials gradually decreased. This may be related to the functional groups of the surfaces of the CQDs (Figs. S1d and S2a-d). Under strongly acidic conditions (e.g., pH = 1), the Zeta potential is the most positive (11.5 mV) which is due to the protonation of the amino groups (NH2) (Scheme 3 a). Whereas under strongly alkaline conditions (e.g., pH = 13), the Zeta potential is the most negative (−16.1 mV) because the dissociation of the surface COOH groups (Scheme 3b). Therefore, at both extremes, the CQDs are more highly charged and thus become hydrophilic and have fewer active surfaces. This is adverse for preparing stable inverse Pickering emulsions.

This is verified by the data for the emulsions prepared at different pH values shown in Figs. S13 and 5b and c. Larger emulsion droplets were formed at pH 1 (360 nm) and pH 13 (680 nm) and the emulsion formed at these pH values were unstable. In contrast, at pH values of 7 and 9, the emulsions were more stable and contained smaller droplets (250 nm, 260 nm). In addition, the emulsion fractions at pH 7 and 9 decreased more slowly over time compared with the other emulsions (Fig. 5c). This is likely due to the better surface activity of the CQDs at pH 7 and 9.

Next the effect of salt concentration on the electrical properties of the CQDs was investigated by measuring Zeta potentials of a series of aqueous CQDs solutions containing different concentrations of NaCl and MgCl2 and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a. For both salts the Zeta potential increased with increasing salt concentration but the CQD solutions containing MgCl2 increased more rapidly and at concentrations above 100 mM a sign change occurred. Clearly Mg2+ with its higher valence had a more significant effect on the Zeta potential. The increase in Zeta potentials with salt concentration may be attributed to the interactions between the counter ions (Na+ or Mg2+) and the electrical double layer. That is at higher concentrations of external electrolytes (NaCl or MgCl2), the counter ions (Na+ or Mg2+) compress the thickness of the CQDs electrical double layer and make it thinner. As a result, more counter ions are squeezed into the inside of the sliding surfaces of the CQDs, which neutralizes the negative charges on the surfaces of the CQDs. This resulted in the Zeta potentials became more positive until they reach zero. The high valence charge and strong adsorption capacity of Mg2+, results in Mg2+ being absorbed by the solvation layer, which leads to the sign change of the Zeta potential.

The addition of salt ions can improve the stability of inverse Pickering emulsions and Kent and Saunders showed that the addition of MgSO4 improved the stability of inverse emulsions with regard to coalescence [46]. Márquez et al. found that the introduction of CaCl2 in the aqueous phase produced more stable W/O emulsions [47]. Zhu et al. demonstrated that in the process of making tofu, the stability of the inverse emulsions was improved by increasing the MgCl2 concentration [48].

In order to investigate this effect, a series of inverse Pickering emulsions with different NaCl and MgCl2 concentrations were prepared and the photos are shown in Figs. S14 and S16. Middle concentrations of salt had lower Zeta potentials (Fig. 6a), which is conducive for the migration of CQDs to the DEP-water interface. This resulted in smaller average droplet sizes (Figs. 6b, 7 and S15) and better emulsion stabilities (Fig. 6c and d). For both NaCl and MgCl2, the smallest average droplet sizes were achieved with salt concentrations of 300 mM. At salt concentrations higher than 300 mM, the average droplet size of the emulsions increased slightly (Figs. 6b, S15g-h and 7i). This may be due to electrostatic screening effects on the surfaces of the CQDs, resulting in flocculation of the CQDs particles which causes the average emulsion droplet size to increase [49].

Moreover, appropriate flocculation is advantageous for forming a stable emulsion (Fig. 6c and d). The addition of Mg2+ significantly improved the stability of the emulsions so that they were stable for more than 4 months (Fig. 8 ). Over time, the emulsions with low MgCl2 concentrations decreased faster than the ones with high MgCl2 concentrations. In comparison, the emulsions containing Na+ were only stable for about 1 month. These results suggest that the high-valence Mg2+ ions underwent more electrostatic repulsions among the charged emulsion droplets, resulting in a reduction in the collision frequency and of the aggregation rate of the droplets which in turn produced more stable inverse Pickering emulsions. It should be noted that the volumes of the resulting emulsions were approximately equal and were not affected by the salt concentration, which demonstrates that the volumes of the resulting emulsions were determined by the volume of the DEP phase and were not significantly influenced by external factors. These results indicate that the addition of salt not only reduced the average droplet size but also increased the emulsion stability, which is in good accordance with the literature [46–48].

In summary, the best conditions for the preparation of inverse Pickering emulsion with good stability and smaller droplets are DEP as organic solvent, sonication time of 15 min, CQDs concentration of 5 mg mL−1, pH 7 (or 9), and concentration of NaCl or MgCl2 being 300 mM.

Ag/CQDs microspheres were synthesized using the inverse Pickering emulsions as templates (Scheme 1B) and the detailed synthesis method for Ag/CQDs microspheres was shown in Supporting Information. The Ag/CQDs emulsion and its optical micrograph are shown in Fig. S17a and c. The Ag/CQDs emulsion droplets were not diluted by water (Fig. S17b), indicating that the emulsion is still inverse type. In order to investigate the composition, morphology, size, functional groups and elemental composition content of the Ag/CQDs microspheres, a series of analyses were carried.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions containing CQDs or Ag/CQDs microspheres are shown in Fig. 9 a. The peaks centered at 340 nm can be attributed to n − π∗ transitions of the CO groups in the aromatic sp2 systems and to the graphitic structure [50–52]. The absorption peak at 429 nm in the Ag/CQDs microsphere spectrum is due to Ag nanoparticles [53]. These results show that the Ag/CQDs microspheres contain both Ag nanoparticles and CQDs.

The XRD pattern of the Ag/CQDs is shown in Fig. 9b. The typical peak for carbon at about 22° is not observed, however, there are four characteristic Ag peaks at 38.2°, 44.1°, 64.4° and 77.5° which correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) crystal planes of face-centered-cubic Ag nanoparticles respectively (JCPDS card NO. 65-8428). This demonstrates that the Ag nanoparticles had good crystallization. The peak observed at 33.7° is likely due to the formation of OCOAg [53,54].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed to analyze the surface chemical composition of the Ag/CQDs microspheres. The full spectrum presented in Fig. 10 a has four typical peaks: C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (399 eV), O 1s (531 eV) and Ag 3d. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 10b) has four peaks at 284.5, 286.2, 287.9, and 288.6 eV, which can be assigned to sp2 carbons (CC), sp3 carbons (CN/CO), carbonyl carbons (CO), and carboxyl carbons (OCOAg), respectively [32,53]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 10c) can be resolved into three peaks at 397.8, 399.3 and 400.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [55]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 10d) contains two peaks centered at 531.6 and 532.8 eV, which are due to CO and OCOAg, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10e, the Ag 3d spectrum has two double peaks. The peaks at lower binding energies 367.6 e V (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.9 eV (Ag 3d3/2) are due to metallic Ag, whereas, the other two peaks centered at 368.4 and 374.4 eV are associated with OCOAg [53].

The synthesized Ag/CQDs microspheres were also characterized by SEM and the results are shown in Fig. 11 a-b. These images show some irregular spherical particles. This may be due to the deformation of the Ag/CQDs microspheres during drying process for preparing SEM samples. Fig. 11c shows the particle size distribution of the Ag/CQDs. The particles ranged in size from 0.3 to 2.5 μm and the average size was 0.61 ± 0.07 μm, which is approximately equal to the average droplet size (less than 1 μm) of the inverse Pickering emulsion.

The microstructure and morphology of the as-prepared Ag/CQDs were also characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 12 a-c, the nearly ball-shaped Ag/CQDs microspheres synthesized using inverse Pickering emulsion templates are composed of central Ag nanoparticles and circumjacent CQDs (Fig. 12b). The diameter of an Ag/CQDs microsphere (Fig. 12a) is about 415 nm, which is consistent with the average size of inverse Pickering emulsion droplet. The HRTEM image of an Ag nanoparticle in Fig. 12c has a lattice fringe spacing of 0.236 nm which corresponds to the (1 1 1) crystal plane, indicating that the Ag nanoparticles had good crystallization. This is in good agreement with the XRD results. EDS spectrum (Fig. 12d) gives the amounts of the four elements. The content of Ag was about 1.52 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalytic performance of Ag/CQDs microspheres for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic performance and reusability of the synthesized Ag/CQDs microspheres were investigated in detail (Supporting Information).

In summary, CQDs were employed as a novel emulsifier to prepare inverse Pickering emulsions. DEP was the most suitable continuous phase solvent for the preparation of the inverse Pickering emulsions. This is due to the “π − π” conjugations and hydrogen bonding interactions between the CQDs and the DEP. The emulsion fraction and the average size of the emulsion droplets depend on various conditions including the ultrasonic time, the CQDs concentration, the DEP/water ratio and the pH. Additionally, the presence of an electrolyte not only significantly increases the stability of inverse Pickering emulsion but also decreases the average emulsion droplet size. The bivalent cation Mg2+ had a significantly larger effect on the stability of the inverse Pickering emulsions than the monovalent cation Na+. The best conditions for the preparation of inverse Pickering emulsion with good stability and smaller droplets are DEP as organic solvent, sonication time of 15 min, CQDs concentration of 5 mg mL−1, pH 7 (or 9), and concentration of NaCl or MgCl2 being 300 mM.

Additionally, a fast and facile one-pot method to synthesize Ag/CQDs microspheres using inverse Pickering emulsions stabilized by CQDs was developed. The synthesized Ag/CQDs microspheres had an average size of less than 1 μm and exhibited excellent catalytic activity for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4. This work not only introduces another application for CQDs but also affords inverse Pickering emulsion templates smaller than 1 μm. This is important for the fabrication of novel CQD-based hybrid materials and various water soluble polymers with different nanostructures.
This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51573126).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.03.075.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Factors that affect Pickering emulsions stabilized by graphene oxide
Formation of hybrid poly(styrene-co-maleic anhydride)–silica microcapsules
Solid nanoparticles that catalyze biofuel upgrade reactions at the water/oil interface
Fast and high-capacity adsorption of Rb+ and Cs+ onto recyclable magnetic porous spheres
Polymerization induced shaping of Pickering emulsion droplets: From simple hollow microspheres to molecularly imprinted multicore microrattles
Compartmentalization of incompatible reagents within Pickering emulsion droplets for one-pot cascade reactions

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: B.P. Binks, T.S. Horozov, Cambridge University Press, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 25th anniversary article: polymer–particle composites: phase stability and applications in electrochemical energy storage
Surface engineering of graphene quantum dots and their applications as efficient surfactants
Macroporous silica from solid-stabilized emulsion templates
Nanoparticle cages for enzyme catalysis in organic media
